Table 1 Generative perplexity (↓) and entropy (↑) across number of function evaluations (NFEs) for LM1B and OWT.
Figure 9 Example OWT generation using CFG (ω = 3.0) and block sampling to generate 4 blocks of size 256. A change in text colour denotes a new block.
Figure 5 Example LM1B generations using 2 and 1024 function evaluations.

Figure 2 Overview of the geometry of the discrete flow map. Left: The instantaneous denoiser ψs,s(xs) at any time s is always on the simplex by weighted sum. Right: By derivation of (19), the mean denoiser ψs,t(xs) is also always a projection onto the simplex, making possible a direct parameterization of the flow map Xs,t that enables our cross-entropy loss functions.
Figure 6 Example OWT generation using CFG (ω = 0.0) and block sampling to generate 4 blocks of size 256. A change in text colour denotes a new block.
Figure 7 Example OWT generation using CFG (ω = 1.0) and block sampling to generate 4 blocks of size 256. A change in text colour denotes a new block.
Table 3 Generative perplexity (↓) and entropy (↑) across number of function evaluations (NFEs).
Figure 8 Example OWT generation using CFG (ω = 2.0) and block sampling to generate 4 blocks of size 256. A change in text colour denotes a new block.

Figure 4 Illustration of block generation with mixed attention masking. A fixed context (green) is processed with causal attention, while a block of future tokens (red) is generated in parallel using bidirectional attention within the block and cross-attention to the context. The attention mask (left) visualizes the masking structure, and the right panel shows how cached key–value (KV) states enable efficient reuse of context while generating successive blocks.
